coredata.dc:title: Strong, tough, and UV-resistant polylactic acid composites by incorporating doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Ling
Liu, Song
Wu, Guoqi

Zhu, Yu
Huang, Weichen
Huang, Haozhe
Peng, Daizhong
Zeng, Shaohua
Guan, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The application of polylactic acid (PLA) is still restricted due to its suboptimal mechanical and thermal properties, as well as its vulnerability to oxidative degradation under ultraviolet light (UV). To enhance these properties, fluorine, nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were fabricated in this study and further employed as nano-reinforcing materials for PLA. Here, FNS-CDs were obtained via the solvothermal method, with citric acid and 3-fluoroaniline serving as carbon, fluorine, and nitrogen sources, while dimethylsulfoxide functioned as the solvent and sulfur source. Subsequently, PLA-based composites were produced by the melt blending technique through blending FNS-CDs with PLA. With the optimal loading of FNS-CDs, the resulting PLA-based composites exhibited remarkable strength and toughness, excellent thermal stability, and efficient UV resistance. Compared with pure PLA, the tensile strength and tensile toughness of PLA-based composites containing 1.5 wt% FNS-CDs increased by 81.4 % and 147.6 % respectively, the crystallinity rose by 25.5 %, and the UV-resistance value was also significantly enhanced. This research demonstrates the considerable potential applications of doped carbon dots in the field of high performance and multi-functional polymer composites.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer composites
Polylactic acid
Carbon dots
Interfacial compatibility

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Postdoctoral Research Project of Anhui Province (Grant No. 2024C940 ), Housing Urban and Rural Construction Science and Technology Plan Project of Anhui Province (Grant No. 2024-YF025 ), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52203077 ). The authors also acknowledge the technical assistance from Anhui Province Engineering Research Center of Critical Electronic Materials and Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Environment-friendly Polymer Materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the preparation process of FNS-CDs and corresponding PLA/CDs composites.
(a) FT-IR and (b) Raman spectra, (c) TEM image, (d) XPS full spectrum of FNS-CDs, and its high-resolution (e) C1s, (f) N1s, (g) F1s, (h) O1s, and (i) S2p spectra.

(a) Tensile stress-strain curves, (b) tensile strength and Young's modulus, (c) tensile toughness, and (d) dielectric loss factor vs. frequency curves of PLA/CDs composites; (e) TEM image of FNS-CDs dispersion in PLA/CDs-0.15 %.
SEM images of fracture surfaces of PLA/CDs composites after tensile tests.
(a) Complex viscosity, (b) storage modulus and (c) loss modulus vs. frequency curves of PLA/CDs composites at 190 °C within a frequency range of 0.01 rad/s to 100 rad/s at 1 % strain.
(a) Second heating DSC curves, (b) TGA and (c) DTG curves of PLA/CDs composites.
(a, b) TGA curves at different heating rates and (c, d) fitting curves under the Kissinger method of PLA and PLA/CDs-0.15 wt%, respectively.
UV absorption spectra of PLA/CDs composites.
Data from the second heating DSC curves and thermal decomposition temperatures of PLA/CDs composites.
Thermal degradation activation energy of pure PLA and PLA/CDs composites.

head.ce:title: Strong, tough, and UV-resistant polylactic acid composites by incorporating doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Anhui University, Hefei 230601, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Municipal and Environmental Engineering, Jilin Jianzhu University, Changchun 130118, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Municipal and Environmental Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Municipal and Environmental Engineering, Jilin Jianzhu University, Changchun 130118, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The authors contributed equally to this work and should be considered co-first authors.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of polylactic acid (PLA) is still restricted due to its suboptimal mechanical and thermal properties, as well as its vulnerability to oxidative degradation under ultraviolet light (UV). To enhance these properties, fluorine, nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were fabricated in this study and further employed as nano-reinforcing materials for PLA. Here, FNS-CDs were obtained via the solvothermal method, with citric acid and 3-fluoroaniline serving as carbon, fluorine, and nitrogen sources, while dimethylsulfoxide functioned as the solvent and sulfur source. Subsequently, PLA-based composites were produced by the melt blending technique through blending FNS-CDs with PLA. With the optimal loading of FNS-CDs, the resulting PLA-based composites exhibited remarkable strength and toughness, excellent thermal stability, and efficient UV resistance. Compared with pure PLA, the tensile strength and tensile toughness of PLA-based composites containing 1.5 wt% FNS-CDs increased by 81.4 % and 147.6 % respectively, the crystallinity rose by 25.5 %, and the UV-resistance value was also significantly enhanced. This research demonstrates the considerable potential applications of doped carbon dots in the field of high performance and multi-functional polymer composites.

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer materials, owing to their lightweight nature, multifunctionality, and low cost, are extensively employed in numerous fields such as food, daily necessities, industry, and agriculture [1–3]. However, traditional polymer materials derived from petroleum give rise to severe environmental pollution issues as they are non-biodegradable. Bio-based polymers originated from natural plant fibers and plant starch have emerged as a research focus as a new type of environmentally friendly and biodegradable plastics [4,5]. Polylactic acid (PLA), a bio-based biodegradable thermoplastic polyester characterized by high strength, excellent processing performance, and high transparency, holds broad application prospects [6–8]. Nevertheless, certain drawbacks of PLA, such as a low crystallization rate and poor mechanical and thermal properties, have restricted its practical applications [9–11]. Additionally, PLA has relatively low resistance to ultraviolet (UV) light and is prone to oxidative degradation when exposed to UV radiation [12,13]. Similar to the modification approaches employed for traditional plastics, the incorporation of nanofillers into PLA through physical blending or chemical polymerization can improve its mechanical and thermal properties, and endow it with additional capabilities such as antimicrobial, fluorescent, and UV-resistance [8,14–16]. The incorporation of nanofillers opens up new possibilities for tailoring the properties of PLA to meet specific application needs and overcome its existing limitations.

Carbon dots (CDs), a novel type of carbon nanomaterials, have remarkable features including low cost, low toxicity, high solubility, photoluminescent properties, and excellent biocompatibility [17–20]. Moreover, the presence of numerous oxygen/nitrogen-containing groups on the CDs' surface, such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, which are highly hydrophilic and beneficial for further modification [21,22], holds great potential for application in the field of reinforced polymers. Kong et al. [23] utilized nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CDs) as cross-linking agents and reinforcing nanofillers to enhance the mechanical strength of neoprene rubber, and the tensile stress and elongation at break of the N-CDs-filled composites were raised to approximately 30 MPa and 1354 MPa, respectively. Xu et al. [24] fabricated flexible nanofibers with uniform diameter and smooth surface via electrostatic spinning of poly(D-propylene crosslinker)/poly(L-lactic acid)/carbon-quantum dots, and such ternary nanofibers showed high piezoelectric response under mechanical stress and presented polychromatic fluorescent effects under light excitation at various wavelengths. Navidfar et al. [25] fabricated three-dimensional nanostructures of graphene nanoparticles, carbon nanotubes, and carbon quantum dots to increase the tensile strength and elastic modulus of polyurethane composites by 54 % and 71 %, respectively.

To enhance the mechanical properties and UV resistance of PLA, the fluorine, nitrogen, sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were ingeniously designed as nano-reinforcing materials in this study. FNS-CDs were successfully synthesized through the solvothermal method, with citric acid and 3-fluoroaniline serving as carbon, fluorine, and nitrogen sources, while dimethylsulfoxide functioned as the solvent and sulfur source. Subsequently, PLA-based composites were produced by blending FNS-CDs nanoparticles with PLA using the melt blending approach. The influences of FNS-CDs on the mechanical, thermal, and UV-resistance properties of PLA-based composites were investigated in detail. Additionally, the reinforcing mechanism and interfacial compatibility of FNS-CDs nanoparticles were contemplated. This study broadens the practical applications of PLA and provides a novel strategy for constructing degradable plastics with excellent UV-resistance, mechanical, and thermal properties.

Citric acid (CA) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were obtained from Shanghai Sinoppharmacy Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., China. 3-fluoroaniline (3-FA) and absolute ethyl alcohol (Abs) were gained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China) and Hubei Xilong Chemical Industry Co. Ltd. (China), respectively. PLA (FY201, Mw ≈ 1.4 × 105 g/mol) was offered by Anhui Fengyuan Polylactic Acid Co. Ltd., China.

The carbon dots were synthesized by a hydrothermal method in a Teflon-lined autoclave, as reported in previous work [26]. Here, CA and 3-FA functioned as the carbon, fluorine, and nitrogen sources, while DMSO acted as both the solvent and sulfur source. The schematic preparation of doped carbon dots is depicted in Fig. 1 . In detail, 2 g of CA and 2 mL of 3-FA were measured and mixed with 30 mL of DMSO in a beaker, followed by 5-min ultrasonication. Subsequently, the dispersion solution was transferred to a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and underwent a reaction at 180 °C for 6 h. After the reaction, the resultant solution in the cooled autoclave was moved into a beaker and 120 mL of Abs was added. Then, the above solution was transferred to the centrifuge tube and centrifuged at 12,000 r/min for 15 min. It was washed with Abs multiple times and centrifuged repeatedly until the supernatant became clear and transparent. Finally, the obtained solid matter was transferred to a vacuum drying oven at 45 °C for drying. This obtained sample was named as FNS-CDs.

PLA-based composites were fabricated by blending FNS-CDs with PLA through the melt blending method [27–29]. PLA pellets were dried in a vented oven at 60 °C overnight to eliminate any moisture before extrusion. Initially, PLA was premixed with FNS-CDs (respectively at 0 wt%, 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.15 wt%, 0.2 wt%, 0.25 wt%). The PLA/CDs composites were fabricated through reaction mixing in a melt mixer at 50 r/min and 180 °C for 10 min. The samples were transferred to molds with diameters of 100 mm × 11 mm × 2 mm, 25 mm and a thickness of 1 mm. The mold was placed in a plate vulcanizing machine under 10 MPa and 180 °C for 10 min for tensile test.

The PLA/CDs composites filled with 0.05 wt%, 0.1 wt%, 0.15 wt%, 0.2 wt%, and 0.25 wt% FNS-CDs were respectively named as PLA/CDs-0.05 %, PLA/CDs-0.1 %, PLA/CDs-0.15 %, PLA/CDs-0.2 %, and PLA/CDs-0.25 %. Pure PLA was also fabricated for comparison.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS; Escalab 250Xi, Thermo Scientific, USA), Fourier infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR; Vertex 80, Bruker, Germany), and Raman spectroscopy (inVia-Reflex, Renishaw, UK) were employed to explore the structure and elemental composition of FNS-CDs. The morphology of FNS-CDs was examined by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, USA). The dispersion of FNS-CDs in the composite was also measured by TEM (JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan). The tensile properties of PLA/CDs composites were determined by a universal electronic tensile machine (CMT-5540, Shenzhen SANS, China) at a speed of 30 mm/min at 25 °C according to ASTM D638. The test specimens were of the dumbbell type, and five specimens were tested for each group. The fracture surfaces of PLA/CDs composites were characterized by a swept surface electron microscope (SEM; S-4800, Hitachi, Japan). The PLA/CDs composites' dielectric loss factor was measured by a broadband dielectric spectrometer (Concept 40, Novocontrol, Germany). The linear rheological behavior of the PLA/CDs composites was ascertained by a rotating plate rheometer (Bohlin Gemini HRnano 200, Malvern, UK) at 190 °C within a frequency range of 0.01 rad/s to 100 rad/s at 1 % strain. The thermal and crystallization behavior of PLA/CDs composites was explored via differential calorimetry (DSC; Instruments Q2000, TA, USA). The temperature was elevated from room temperature to 200 °C in an N2 atmosphere at a rate of 10 °C/min, maintained for 5 min, and subsequently decreased to room temperature at 10 °C/min, with the heat flow curves being recorded; three specimens were tested for each group. The thermal stability of the composites was inspected by employing a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA; Q500IR, TA, USA), and the tests were conducted in a nitrogen atmosphere within the temperature range of 30 °C to 600 °C. The optical properties of the samples were observed through characterizing the transmittance of the composites with UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV-3600, Shimadzu, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and morphology of doped carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: The structure and morphology of FNS-CDs were analyzed by means of FT-IR, Raman spectroscopy, XPS, and TEM technologies, and the corresponding results are presented in Fig. 2 . As depicted in Fig. 2(a), a broad and prominent peak emerges at approximately 3500 cm−1, which is assumed to be the stretching vibration peak of hydroxyl groups or amino groups. Meanwhile, distinct absorption peaks are observed at 1692 cm−1, 1377 cm−1, 1242 cm−1, and 1172 cm−1, which are inferred to be the stretching vibration peaks of carbonyl groups (C=O), CN, CF, and CS respectively. It can be identified from Fig. 2(b) that there are two intense peaks at the D peak of 1390 cm−1 and the G peak of 1602 cm−1. Among them, the intensity ratio between the D peak and the G peak (ID/IG = 0.78) determines the defects of the carbon atom crystal, that is, the larger the ID/IG, the more defects there are in the carbon atom crystal [26,30]. Due to the considerable ID/IG, the FNS-CDs prepared in the experiment have numerous defects, which also indicates that the surface of FNS-CDs contains a large number of functional groups (e.g., hydroxyl groups and carbonyl groups). The well dispersion of FNS-CDs is clearly demonstrated by TEM, as presented in Fig. 2(c). The FNS-CDs along with a particle size range of 2.5 nm to 8.5 nm (see Fig. S1) are evenly distributed without any significant aggregation or clustering, thus offering strong evidence for their excellent dispersibility.

The peak position and peak shape of the XPS map typically allow for obtaining the element composition, chemical state, and molecular structure of the sample surface, while the peak intensity assists in determining the element content or concentration of the sample surface. Fig. 2(d) presents four peaks at 284.8 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.7 eV, and 288.6 eV, corresponding to C-C/C=C, C-N/C-O/C-S, C=O/C=N, and CF [31]. In Fig. 2(f), the total peak of N 1 s can be decomposed into three peaks, respectively located at 398.6 eV (pyridinic-N), 399.5 eV (aniline-N), and 400.5 eV (pyrrolic-N) [32]. Meanwhile, it can be observed from Fig. 2(g) that there is a sole peak at 686.3 eV, which is supposed to be CF. In Fig. 2(h), the total peak of O 1 s can be divided into three peaks, positioned at 531.3 eV (C=O), 532.1 eV (CO), and 533.2 eV (O=C-O) [33]. Fig. 2(i) indicates the existence of the C-S-C structure in the prepared carbon dots, attributed to the involvement of DMSO as a solvent in the hydrothermal reaction [31]. These results suggest that the F, N, and S elements have been successfully introduced to the surface of CDs, and hydroxyl and amino groups are present on the surface of CDs.

Fig. 3 (a–c) presents the mechanical characteristics of PLA/CDs composites with varying FNS-CDs contents. As shown in Fig. 3(b, c), the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and tensile toughness of PLA/CDs composites increase as the FNS-CDs content rises. However, once the content of FNS-CDs exceeds 0.15 wt%, the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and tensile toughness of PLA/CDs composites begin to decline. The mechanical properties of PLA/CDs composites attain their peak level when the content of FNS-CDs is 0.15 wt%; the tensile strength, Young's modulus, and tensile toughness increase by 81.4 %, 55.4 %, and 147.6 % respectively, compared with those of pure PLA. These outcomes firmly validate that FNS-CDs have the capability to significantly enhance the strength, modulus, and toughness of PLA-based composites. The introduction of FNS-CDs plays a significant role in multiple aspects: firstly, FNS-CDs function as a nano-enhancement of the nano-filler itself, effectively improving the inherent properties of composites; moreover, owing to the introduction of hydrophobic elements (F, N, S), a robust interfacial interaction (rather than chemical bonding) is formed between FNS-CDs and PLA matrix [34], which promotes more efficient energy transfer. The strengthening and toughening effects of FNS-CDs on PLA matrix is significantly superior to those of N-doped CDs [35,36] or N,S-doped CDs [37]. The relevant reports on the utilization of CDs to enhance PLA are listed in Table S1. Nevertheless, when the content of FNS-CDs exceeds the appropriate level, the aggregation of FNS-CDs within the PLA matrix takes place, which subsequently leads to stress concentration and an escalation in defect points. This, in turn, causes a substantial decline in the mechanical properties of PLA/CDs composites.

The interfacial polarization of composites can be regulated by the dispersion of internal nanofillers and interfacial interactions within them [38,39]. The outstanding dispersion of nanofillers and the extremely robust interfacial compatibility between nanofiller and matrix play a vital role in prominently reducing the internal defects of the composites and effectively minimizing the interfacial polarization. Fig. 3(d) presents the dielectric loss factor (Tanδ) in relation to frequency for PLA/CDs composites. The Tanδ of PLA/CDs composites initially decreases and then increases as the frequency rises. The Tanδ of PLA/CDs composites at low frequencies (100–103 Hz) is significantly lower than that of pure PLA. The reduced Tanδ of PLA/CDs composites implies that FNS-CDs exhibit an outstanding degree of dispersion and a strong interfacial compatibility with the PLA matrix.

Fig. 3(e) presents the dispersion state of FNS-CDs within the PLA matrix. It is quite evident that the well-dispersed FNS-CDs can be clearly observed in PLA/CDs-0.15 %. The uniform dispersion of these FNS-CDs in PLA matrix indicates a homogeneous mixture between FNS-CDs and PLA matrix, which potentially contributes to the enhanced mechanical properties.

To investigate the composites' interfacial compatibility more effectively, the fracture surfaces of PLA/CDs composites were inspected as shown in Fig. 4 . In the absence of FNS-CDs, pure PLA displays a relatively smooth cross-section, which is consistent with the considerable brittleness of PLA itself. With 0.15 wt% FNS-CDs incorporated into the PLA matrix, the occurrence of folding and wire drawing increase, indicating that the carbon dots can be uniformly dispersed in the PLA matrix. When the filling amount of FNS-CDs is 0.2 wt%, a large number of rough folds emerge on the section, and the section is obviously stratified, presenting wire drawing and dimpled phenomena. This structure implies that when the composite undergoes tensile stress, the uniformly dispersed FNS-CDs are beneficial for promoting stress transfer and thereby absorbing more energy, enabling the composite with better toughness and strength. When the FNS-CDs content reaches 0.25 wt%, an excessive amount of FNS-CDs is formed, and some FNS-CDs aggregates are observed on the surface of the PLA matrix. These FNS-CDs aggregates are likely to cause stress concentration and aggravate the fracture of the composites. Therefore, appropriate FNS-CDs addition is beneficial for strengthening and toughening PLA-based composites.

Rheological behavior serves as a crucial indicator for assessing the processing performance of polymer composites [40–43]. Fig. 5 depicts the variations of the viscosity (η*), energy storage modulus (G′), and loss modulus (G″) versus frequency curves of PLA/CDs composites. As shown in Fig. 5(a), with the increase of shear frequency, the η* of the composites gradually decreases, and this decreasing trend is more pronounced for the PLA-based composites with the addition of 0.15 wt% FNS-CDs. This phenomenon can be attributed to the disentanglement and reorientation of the FNS-CDs and PLA polymer chains in the flow direction, thereby reducing the viscous drag and presenting a shear-thinning characteristic. Moreover, the η* of PLA/CDs composites rises when FNS-CDs are incorporated at low frequencies. The integration of FNS-CDs results in a strong interfacial interaction between PLA and FNS-CDs and efficient stress transfer, both of which enhance the η* and G′ [44]. PLA/CDs-0.15 % exhibits the maximum G′ values at 101 Hz, indicating the strongest interfacial interaction between PLA and FNS-CDs. A similar trend is observed for the G″ of PLA/CDs composites. The addition of FNS-CDs into the PLA matrix restricts the movement of PLA chains, leading to an increase in G″. Furthermore, the G′ values of the composites are consistently higher than G″ values across the entire frequency range. This solid-like behavior implies that the elastic fraction outweighs the viscous fraction, resulting in a reduction of interfacial energy dissipation [45].

The crystallization behavior of the PLA matrix was investigated by DSC. The second heating DSC curves of PLA/CDs composites are presented in Fig. 6(a), and the cold crystallization enthalpy (∆Hc), melting enthalpy (∆Hm), and crystalline content (Xc) are listed in Table 1 . For pure PLA, the Xc value is 50.88 %. As the content of FNS-CDs increases, Xc values of PLA/CDs composites initially rise and then decline. When 0.15 wt% FNS-CDs is incorporated, the Xc value of the composite increases to 76.39 %, which is 25.51 % higher than that of pure PLA. This phenomenon indicates that FNS-CDs have a nucleating effect on the crystallization of PLA. However, when an excessive amount of FNS-CDs is added, the dispersion of FNS-CDs in the matrix deteriorates, and its aggregates tend to be not fully covered by the PLA matrix, thereby reducing the crystallization of the composites. Primarily, the well-distributed FNS-CDs can enhance the crystallization efficiency of PLA.

The thermal stability of PLA/CDs composites was evaluated by TGA as assessed by TGA, as depicted in Fig. 6(b, c). The decomposition temperature and residual weight of the composites are also presented in Table 1. It can be observed from Fig. 6(b) that both pure PLA and PLA/CDs composites possess only one decomposition stage, which is an inherent characteristic of PLA. The maximum thermal degradation temperature (Tmax) of pure PLA is 376.7 °C, while that of the PLA/CDs composites is marginally lower. This is attributed to the lower thermal decomposition temperature of FNS-CDs. Nevertheless, the highest Tmax of the composites is observed when FNS-CDs is added at 0.15 wt%, suggesting that PLA/CDs-0.15 wt% exhibits better thermal stability. This can be associated with the uniform distribution of FNS-CDs in the PLA matrix and favorable interfacial compatibility. Notably, the addition of FNS-CDs increases the residual weight of PLA at 600 °C, indicating that the presence of FNS-CDs facilitates the formation of a carbon layer on the surface of PLA, thereby impeding further thermal decomposition.

To further explore the influence of FNS-CDs on the thermal stability of PLA matrix, the thermal degradation behavior of pure PLA and PLA/CDs composites were analyzed by TGA at different heating rates. The Kissinger model [46] was established to assess the variation of activation energy of thermal degradation (EKissinger) at various levels of thermal conversion, thereby facilitating the prediction of the composites' stability. The TGA curves of PLA and PLA/CDs-0.15 % at diverse heating rates are respectively presented in Fig. 7 (a, b). During the thermal decomposition process, the TGA curves shift to higher temperatures as the heating rate increases. The variation of the TGA curves can be ascribed to the disparity in the heat transfer and kinetic energy rates, indicating that the gasification rate of the reaction products might be decelerated with the increase of the heating rate [47]. At higher rates, the shorter the time needed to reach the decomposition temperature, the greater the temperature difference between the interior and exterior of the sample, which delays the decomposition [48]. Fig. 7(c, d) presents the linear fitting curves of pure PLA and PLA/CDs-0.15 % through the Kissinger algorithm, and Table 2 enumerates the EKissinger for different heating rates. It can be observed from Table 2 that the EKissinger of PLA/CDs-0.15 % rises from 55.0 kJ/mol (for pure PLA) to 58.2 kJ/mol. This finding implies that the thermal degradation rate of PLA/CDs composites decelerates with the addition of FNS-CDs. FNS-CDs exert a certain inhibitory effect on the thermal degradation of PLA matrix.

Ultraviolet light can be classified into three regions based on wavelengths, namely long-wave ultraviolet (UV-A) ranging from 320 to 400 nm, medium-wave ultraviolet (UV-B) spanning 280 to 320 nm, and short-wave ultraviolet (UV-C) covering 200 to 280 nm [49,50]. The UV absorption properties of PLA/CDs composites are presented in Fig. 8 . Pure PLA exhibits absorption values nearly zero in both UV-A and UV-B, suggesting a weak UV absorption capacity [51]. Since UV-C is readily blocked by the atmosphere, PLA-based composites that absorb UV-B and UV-A (i.e., UV with wavelengths exceeding 280 nm) can expand their application in food packaging, agriculture, etc. [52,53]. When FNS-CDs are incorporated, the UV absorption values of PLA/CDs composites within the UV-A and UV-B ranges are significantly increased. Carbon dots can improve the UV resistance of PLA due to their distinctive UV absorption characteristics [54,55]. Interestingly, the absorption peak of PLA/CDs composites at 280 nm is clearly distinguishable (Fig. 8). This absorption intensity is raised from 0.03 for pure PLA to 0.28–0.43 for PLA/CDs composites. The rising absorption intensity is mainly attributed to the n → π* transitions of the heteroatoms F, N, and S on the CDs surface. Furthermore, in comparison with pure PLA, an absorption effect within the wavelength range of 400 to 600 nm (visible region) for PLA/CDs composites is still observed, which is higher than that of PLA-based composites containing F,N-doped CDs [34]. This result further substantiates the positive impact of S doping on the n → π* transitions. Consequently, the addition of FNS-CDs results in superior UV resistance of PLA/CDs composites, expanding the application scope of PLA-based composites.

In this research, fluorine, nitrogen, and sulfur-doped carbon dots (FNS-CDs) were fabricated via the solvothermal approach and subsequently utilized as nano-reinforcing fillers to enhance the properties of PLA-based composites. PLA-based composites were fabricated by blending FNS-CDs nanoparticles with PLA through the melt blending method. Mechanical tests revealed that the PLA/CDs-0.15 wt% samples exhibited strong interfacial interactions. Compared with pure PLA, the tensile strength and tensile toughness increased by 81.4 % and 147.6 % respectively. Thermal analysis indicated that an appropriate amount of FNS-CDs nanoparticles could act as heterogeneous nucleation agents and enhance the crystallinity of PLA. The increased activation energy for thermal degradation of the composites demonstrated that FNS-CDs nanoparticles effectively inhibited the thermal degradation of PLA. Meanwhile, the PLA/CDs composites displayed excellent absorption capabilities in the UV region.

Ling Sun: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Software, Data curation. Song Liu: Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Guoqi Wu: Visualization, Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Huangzhe Shen: Software, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yu Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Weichen Huang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Haozhe Huang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Daizhong Peng: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shaohua Zeng: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Jun Guan: Supervision, Resources, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Postdoctoral Research Project of Anhui Province (Grant No. 2024C940), Housing Urban and Rural Construction Science and Technology Plan Project of Anhui Province (Grant No. 2024-YF025), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52203077). The authors also acknowledge the technical assistance from Anhui Province Engineering Research Center of Critical Electronic Materials and Anhui Province Key Laboratory of Environment-friendly Polymer Materials.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142416.
